file_name: WOMENSGOLFUNLIMITEDINC_03_29_2000-EX-10.13-ENDORSEMENT AGREEMENT - Intellectual Property Rights                 Confidentiality and Non-Use Obligations Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be executed in two or more counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original, but all of which together shall constitute one and the same instrument.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement pertains to marketing strategies, golf equipment designs, and computer software. This Agreement may be executed in two or more counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original, but all of which together shall constitute one and the same instrument.
perturbation.explanation: The counterparts clause, ensuring that multiple signed copies form one agreement, is moved to a location suggesting it only applies to the listed topics. This could create ambiguity about whether the clause applies to the entire agreement or only specific sections. Such displacement of material information negatively effects the whole of the contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: UETA § 5 addresses the use of electronic records and signatures and states that a record or signature may not be denied legal effect or enforceability solely because it is in electronic form. By placing the counterparts clause, which reinforces the validity of multiple agreement copies, in a section that seems to relate only to specific subject matter (marketing, equipment, software), doubt is cast on whether this rule applies to the agreement in general, potentially contradicting UETA's broad acceptance of electronic records.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Archives & Special Collections • Library • Penn Carey Law Skip to main content Home Library » Archives & Special Collections Archives & Special Collections The Archives and Special Collections Department of Biddle Law Library preserves, promotes, and provides access to the papers and records of two major legal organizations: The American Law Institute (ALI), and the National Bankruptcy Archives. The Department also cares for a collection of rare books, manuscripts, and archives from the University of Pennsylvania Carey Law School. Search Center Skip Sidebar Navigation Library Expand mobile section navigation Research Using The Library Faculty Services Archives & Special Collections About the Library News Library Map Library Hours Contact Us Collections The Archives of the American Law Institute (ALI) The ALI was established in 1923 by judges, lawyers, and law professors in an effort to promote the clarification, simplification, and scholarship of the law. The ALI Archives contains over 800 cubic feet of material documenting the administration, organization and work of the Institute, from early projects like the restatement of basic legal subjects to a collaboration with NCCUSL in developing and monitoring the Uniform Commercial Code. View more information about the ALI collections The National Bankruptcy Archives (NBA) In 2000, the Biddle Law Library and the American College of Bankruptcy collaborated to establish the National Bankruptcy Archives (NBA), a national repository of materials relating to the history of debtor-creditor relations, bankruptcy, and the reorganization of debt. The NBA currently contains over 1,000 linear feet of personal papers and institutional records. View more information about the NBA collections Explore the NBA Oral History collections Biddle Collections The Collections consist of manuscript collections, rare books, oral histories, and a select number of Penn Carey Law School’s records. The manuscript collections include the papers of George Wharton Pepper, Bernie Segal, William Draper Lewis, and David L. Bazelon. The Raymond F. Trent Collection consists of books, periodicals, articles, and audio tapes concerning the history of education and practice of Black lawyers in the United States. Some records relating to the operations of Penn Carey Law School are maintained by the University Archives . Please contact us for more information. View more information about the Biddle Law Library Collections Explore the collection of The Report the Law School’s yearbook Explore the Penn Carey Law Oral History Project collection Contact & Hours The Archives and Special Collections office is located on the fourth floor of Tanenbaum Hall. The mailing address is: University of Pennsylvania Carey Law School Biddle Law Library - Archives Department 3501 Sansom Street Philadelphia, PA 19104-3406 For questions about the Archives, the Rare Books Collection, or other special collections, please email the Archives Department at biddlearchives@law.upenn.edu . The Archives is open Tuesday-Friday, 10AM to 4PM. Appointments should be made 2 weeks in advance of the visit. Visitors will be asked to show identification. Policies Registration All researchers are asked to fill out our Researcher Registration Form. This form can be filled out prior to your visit or on the day of your arrival. Archives - Researcher Registration Form On-site Research Items in the Archives do not circulate. If you wish to come to the Archives to conduct research, you may do so free of charge. However, you must contact the department ahead of time to schedule an appointment. Appointments should be made 2 weeks in advance of desired research date. Visitors to the Law School will be asked to show identification. Remote Research For patrons who cannot travel to the Law School to conduct research, the Archives will assist in the identification of potentially relevant materials for your research topic or for photocopying. We do not transcribe, paraphrase,
perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation states the counterparts clause, which ensures that multiple signed copies form one agreement, is moved to a location suggesting it only applies to the listed topics. The law explanation states that doubt is cast on whether this rule applies to the agreement in general. This aligns with the definition of a Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction, where a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section. Although the scraped snippets do not directly support this, the definition itself is met and thus the change does create a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Professional acknowledges her obligations under the provisions of the Intellectual Property Rights Confidentiality and Non-Use Obligations Agreement attached hereto as "Exhibit A" and made a part hereof by this reference. The rights and obligations of the parties set forth in Exhibit A shall survive the termination or expiration of this endorsement agreement, regardless of cause or circumstances of the termination or expiration.
perturbation.changed_text: The Professional acknowledges that Exhibit A exists. The Professional acknowledges her obligations under the provisions of the Intellectual Property Rights Confidentiality and Non-Use Obligations Agreement attached hereto as "Exhibit A" and made a part hereof by this reference.
perturbation.explanation: Deleting the sentence stating the rights and obligations in Exhibit A survive termination weakens the enforceability of the intellectual property rights and confidentiality obligations. It introduces ambiguity about whether these obligations continue after the contract ends, which is vital for protecting the company's assets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New Jersey Trade Secrets Act, N.J.S.A. 56:15-1 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: The New Jersey Trade Secrets Act protects trade secrets even after the termination of a relationship. By removing the clause that explicitly states the confidentiality obligations survive termination, the modified agreement becomes less clear about the continued protection of the Company's trade secrets. This weakening of the clause could be interpreted as contradicting the intent of the N.J. Trade Secrets Act, which aims to protect trade secrets even after the business relationship has ended.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit statement that confidentiality obligations survive termination, weakening the protection of trade secrets after the contract ends. The New Jersey Trade Secrets Act, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1 (NJ Rev Stat § 56:15-1), aims to protect trade secrets. By omitting the clause ensuring the survival of confidentiality, the modified agreement contradicts the intent of the Act to protect trade secrets even after the termination of the business relationship.
